coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of multi-color fluorescent carbon quantum dots and solid state CQDs@SiO2 nanophosphors for light-emitting devices
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2019 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l.
coredata.dc:creator: Song, Xiaoqian
Guo, Qianyi
Cai, Zhenlu
Qiu, Jianrong
Dong, Guoping
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with multi-color emission are synthesized through hydrothermal method by varying the ratio of citric acid (CA) and urea, the solvent (water, ethyl alcohol, and dimethylformamide), the hydrothermal temperature, and reaction time. The CQDs emit bright fluorescence which can be gradually tuned from blue to red continuously, almost covering the whole visible spectrum. The emission peaks of the CQDs shift from 450 nm to 650 nm with the surface groups and structures changing. Importantly, all solid state CQDs@SiO2 nanophosphors are successfully synthesized by reversed-phase micromulsion method. Based on these solid state phosphors, multi-color CQDs@SiO2 phosphors-based light-emitting diodes (LED) and white light-emitting diode (WLED) with a Commission Internationale de L’ Eclairage (CIE) value of (0.3497, 0.3045) are fabricated in a low-cost, environment-friendly way.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photoluminescence
Carbon quantum dots
Multi-color
Light-emitting diode

The properties of solid state CQDs@SiO2 phosphors

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of B-CQDs (a), G-CQDs (b), Y-CQDs (c), O-CQDs (d), and R-CQDs (e), respectively. f) FTIR spectra of the CQDs samples. g) Raman spectra of the CQDs with a 785 nm laser as the excitation source.

a) PL emission spectra (λem = 365 nm) of B-CQD, G-CQDs, Y-CQDs, O-CQDs, and R-CQDs. b) PL emission spectra of B-CQDs, G-CQDs, Y-CQDs, O-CQDs, and R-CQDs excited at different wavelengths. (c) The excitation spectra of the CQDs. (d) The UV–vis absorption spectra of the CQDs. (e) The decay curves of the CQDs under 365 nm excitation. Photographs of the CQDs solution samples under daylight (f) and 365 nm UV light (g).

a) EPR spectra of the CQDs. b) Emission wavelength, λem of CQDs v.s. EPR signal intensity, IEPR of CQDs. c) Mechanism of multi-color emitting CQDs with the increase of surface oxidation and carboxyl. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

a) Photographs of the multi-color emitting CQDs@SiO2 phosphors under 365 nm UV light. b) PL emission spectra of the multi-color emitting CQDs@SiO2 phosphors and their corresponding CQDs solution. TEM images of Y-CQDs@SiO2 phosphors with TEOS dosage at 5 μL (c), 10 μL (d), and 15 μL (e), respectively. The insets are their corresponding size distribution histograms, which show that the average size of Y-CQDs@SiO2 phosphors becomes larger from 18 nm to 68 nm, and 160 nm. f) XRD patterns of Y-CQDs@SiO2 phosphors with different TEOS dosages. g) FTIR spectra of Y-CQDs and Y-CQDs@SiO2 phosphors with different TEOS dosages. h) PL emission spectra of Y-CQDs and Y-CQDs@SiO2 phosphors with different TEOS dosages. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Photographs (a), emission spectra (b), and the CIE color coordinates (c) of the multi-color CQDs@SiO2 phosphors-based LEDs. The different color CQDs@SiO2 phosphors-based LEDs using B, G, Y, O, and R-CQDs@SiO2 phosphors, respectively. Emission spectrum (d), and the CIE color coordinate (e) of the solid state CQDs@SiO2 phosphors-based WLED. The inset in (d) is the photograph of the operating WLED. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Schematic illustration of the synthesis process of CQDs.
Hydrothermal reaction parameters of the CQDs.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of multi-color fluorescent carbon quantum dots and solid state CQDs@SiO2 nanophosphors for light-emitting devices

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Optical Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Optical Science and Engineering, State Key Laboratory of Modern Optical Instrumentation, Zhejiang University, Hangzhou, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with multi-color emission are synthesized through hydrothermal method by varying the ratio of citric acid (CA) and urea, the solvent (water, ethyl alcohol, and dimethylformamide), the hydrothermal temperature, and reaction time. The CQDs emit bright fluorescence which can be gradually tuned from blue to red continuously, almost covering the whole visible spectrum. The emission peaks of the CQDs shift from 450 nm to 650 nm with the surface groups and structures changing. Importantly, all solid state CQDs@SiO2 nanophosphors are successfully synthesized by reversed-phase micromulsion method. Based on these solid state phosphors, multi-color CQDs@SiO2 phosphors-based light-emitting diodes (LED) and white light-emitting diode (WLED) with a Commission Internationale de L’ Eclairage (CIE) value of (0.3497, 0.3045) are fabricated in a low-cost, environment-friendly way.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have drawn much attention owing to their outstanding features, such as photostability, chemical stability, biocompatibility, low toxicity, and low cost [1–5]. Therefore, CQDs are widely studied for potential applications in light-emitting devices (LED) [6,7], photocatalysis [8], biosensors [9], and etc. In order to meet the above potential applications, it is necessary to prepare CQDs with multi-color emission. Recently, CQDs with different color emissions have been tried to synthesize. Yang et al. modified the surface of CQDs by amidation, leading to blue shift in a short wavelength range [10]. Tessuka et al. prepared nitrogen-rich CQDs through hydrothermal method and obtained blue, green, and yellow CQDs by changing reaction conditions [11]. Jeon el al. regulated the emission peak red-shift of CQDs by aminating the surface of CQDs [12]. To date, although multi-color emitting CQDs have been studied, the preparation methods are usually complex and the tunable multi-color emission is difficult to cover the whole visible spectrum. Therefore, it is still significant to find a low-cost and environment-friendly way to prepare multi-color fluorescent CQDs.

Nowadays, phosphor-based LED is one of the most popular research fields. Rare earth ion-based LEDs occupy the main position, but the raw materials are expensive [13–15]. Another main phosphor material for LED is quantum dots (QDs), which possess high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) and narrow full-width at half maximum (FWHM) [16,17]. However, the toxicity of cadmium and lead ions cannot be overlooked for application in LEDs [18]. Therefore, LED materials with excellent fluorescence, good stability, low cost, and low toxicity are in urgent need. According to these requirements, CQD-based phosphors with excellent luminescence properties are one of the ideal candidates for LEDs. However, CQDs usually show aggregation-induced luminescence quenching, resulting in weak or quenching photoluminescence in solid state [19,20]. So, almost all previous works about CQDs were limited in solution or liquid state, which severely preventing the development and applications of CQDs. Therefore, solid state CQD-based phosphors with multi-color emissions are urgently required. Wang et al. Obtained solid state blue CQD-based phosphors with polyhedral oligomeric silsesquioxane matrix [21]. Zhou et al. realized solid state green emission CQD-based phosphors by incorporating CQDs within starch, and barium sulfate [22,23]. In this work, we have successfully obtained solid state CQDs@SiO2 phosphors with multi-color emissions in a low-cost and environment-friendly way.

Herein, we synthesized almost full-color fluorescent CQDs through facial one-step hydrothermal method. The emission peak gradually shifts from 450 nm to 650 nm with the adjustment of reaction conditions, such as hydrothermal temperature, reaction time, ratio of critic acid (CA) and urea, and solvent. By using silica to encapsulate the CQDs, solid state CQDs@SiO2 phosphors with multi-color emissions were successfully obtained through “reversed-phase micromulsion” preparation method. Abundant hydroxyl groups on the surface of CQDs react with TEOS, obtaining the CQDs coated with SiO2. CQDs@SiO2 phosphors were prepared remaining the surface state environments of CQDs unchanged. This preparation method is critical and effective to avoid the aggregation-induced luminescence quenching of CQDs. The size of Y-CQDs@SiO2 phosphors distributes in a large range (18–159 nm) with different TEOS dosages, and the phosphors possess good dispersity, which can be put into various application fields. Subsequently, multi-color LEDs and WLED were fabricated using the above multi-color fluorescent CQDs@SiO2 phosphors. By tuning the content ratio of different color CQDs@SiO2 phosphors, WLED with a Commission Internationale de L'Eclairage (CIE) value of (0.3497, 0.3045) was successfully fabricated.

Critic acid monohydrate (AR), Urea (AR), N,N-Dimethylformamide (AR), acetone (AR), and Ethyl alcohol (AR) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent (SCR) Co., Ltd, Shanghai, China. Hexamethylene (AR), Ammonium hydroxide (AR), Igepal CO-520, Tetraethyl orthosilicate (AR), and 3-Aminopropyltriethoxysilane (AR) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Deionized water was used throughout the work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of solid state CQDs@SiO2 phosphors
ce:section.ce:para: 10 μL Tetraethyl Orthosilicate (TEOS) was added into 1500 μL as-prepared CQDs solution and stirred for 1 h at room temperature, and then the CQDs became silanized. 10 mL hexamethylene and 1 g Igepal CO-520 were mixed together and the solution was magnetically stirred for 10 min. 300 μL silanized CQDs and x μL (x = 5, 10, 15) TEOS were injected into the solution and stirred for 1 h. Then, 300 μL 8.3% ammonium hydroxide and 180 μL 3-Aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APS) were added in turn and kept stirring for 1 h. At last, 10 mL acetone was gradually added for demulsification, the solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min by 3 times. Abundant hydroxyl groups on the surface of CQDs react with TEOS, obtaining the CQDs coated with SiO2. Ultimately, the CQDs@SiO2 phosphors were obtained after drying for 12 h at 50 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of solid state CQDs@SiO2 phosphor-based LED
ce:section.ce:para: 0.1 g of silicone resin A and 0.05 g of silicone B were mixed with 0.1 g CQDs@SiO2 phosphors, the mixture was stirred thoroughly. Then, the mixture was deposited on a led chip. Afterward, the LED device was cured at 150 °C for 1.5 h. Finally, solid state CQDs@SiO2 phosphor-based LEDs were fabricated.

The photoluminescence (PL) spectra and lifetime decay curves were investigated on a high resolution spectrofluorometer (Edinburgh Instruments FLS 920) equipped with a single photon counting photomultiplier (Hamamatsu R928P). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained on a Nicolette 6700 FTIR spectrophotometer in the region from 4000 to 400 cm−1. Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images were investigated using TEM 2100F (JEOL, Japan). The absorption spectra were measured in the range of 300–700 nm using a PerkinElmer Lambda 900/UV/VIS/NIR spectrophotometer. The Raman spectra were measured using a Raman spectrometer (Renishaw in Via, Gloucestershire, UK) with a 785 nm laser as the excitation source. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the products were recorded on a D8 advance X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, Switzerland) using Cu Ka radiation (λ = 1.54060 Å). The electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra were investigated by Bruker ELEXSYS-II E500. The optical properties of LEDs were obtained using an HAAS2000 integrated sphere with an analyzer system of EVERFINE.

The optical properties of the multi-color emitting CQD solution samples were accessed by PL emission spectra under an excitation wavelength of 365 nm. Fig. 2(f–g) show the photographs of the selected multi-color emitting CQD solution samples under daylight and 365 nm UV lamp, respectively. Under UV light, the CQD solution samples show obvious blue, green, yellow, orange, and red emissions. Fig. 2 (a) shows the emission spectra of B-CQDs, G-CQDs, Y-CQDs, O-CQDs, and R-CQDs, respectively. The emission peak gradually shifts from 450 nm (blue) to 650 nm (red), almost covering the whole visible spectrum. The PL emission spectra of the CQDs solutions excited at different wavelengths is shown in Fig. 2(b). With the red-shift of the excitation wavelength, the CQDs fluorescent intensity was strengthened and then weakened, and the emission peaks gradually red shift, revealing that the CQDs possess excitation wavelength-dependent emission properties. The excitation wavelengths of maximum emission intensity are about 365 nm, 400 nm, 460 nm, 480 nm, and 480 nm, respectively. Series of energy levels between π-π* are formed because of various groups on CQDs surface, generating different luminescent centers. When the excitation wavelength changes, the corresponding luminescent center dominates, which results in the excitation wavelength-dependent emission properties [29]. The excitation spectra (Fig. 2(c)) and UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. 2(d)) demonstrate that the CQDs show corresponding excitonic absorption bands. In Fig. 2(c), the optimum excitation wavelengths of the CQDs are 365 nm, 390 nm, 460 nm, 470 nm, and 480 nm, agreeing well with the results in Fig. 2(b). The B-CQDs show an absorption band at 340 nm, connected with the π-π* transition of conjugated C=O and C=N [30,31]. G-CQDs and Y-CQDs possess extra bands at about 450 nm. As for O-CQDs and R-CQDs, absorption peaks are observed at about 550 nm attributed to the π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 system [32]. The different absorption bands of CQDs prove that the multi-color emitting CQDs possess diverse surface states. The decay curves of the CQDs solution samples are depicted in Fig. 2(e). By the monoexponential fitting, it can be estimated that the fluorescence lifetimes of the CQDs solution are 4.63 ns (B-CQDs), 5.23 ns (G-CQDs), 5.78 ns (Y-CQDs), 1.78 ns (O-CQDs), and 1.49 ns (R-CQDs), respectively. The fluorescent lifetimes of O-CQDs and R-CQDs are quite short, demonstrating that they probably possess a large amount of surface defects. CQDs use surface defects as excited energy traps for radiative recombination, resulting in luminescence. It can be found that CQDs samples in the same solvent exhibit similar fluorescent lifetimes. On the one hand, surface groups of CQDs affect the fluorescent lifetime. On the other hand, polarity of solvents also makes an impact. The polarity of H2O is greater than that of DMF. In exception, ethanol has lower polarity, but it is protonic solvent, which hinders the excited-state intramolecular proton transfer (ESIPT). Therefore, the fluorescent lifetimes of G-CQDs and Y-CQDs synthesized in ethanol are distinctly longer than B-CQDs synthesized in H2O and O-CQDs and R-CQDs synthesized in DMF.

In previous reports, two main mechanisms of tunable emitting CQDs have been proposed. One is the effect of CQDs particle size, the other is surface defects [10,33]. In our present work, multi-color emission CQDs possess similar particle sizes, simultaneously the FTIR spectra show that the carboxyl and oxidation degree increase with the red shift emission of CQDs. Thus, it can be deduced that the PL mechanism of multi-color emitting CQDs is mainly related with surface defects state. EPR is one of the powerful methods to study the defects of luminescent materials. Fig. 3 (a) shows the EPR spectra of CQDs, the five samples possess similar lorentzian shape. The g factor is about 2.003, which proving the existence of singly occupied orbitals. In Fig. 3(b), with the red shift emission of the CQDs, the EPR signal intensity increases, and the amount of unpaired electron is more. It can be seen in the TEM results in Fig. 1(a–e) that the size of CQDs are similar, so it can be deduced that the increase of EPR signal intensity is mainly ascribed to the increase of CQD surface defects. Moreover, slopes of IEPR/λem between CQDs synthesized in the same solvent is smaller than those in different solvents, indicating that surface groups formed in different solvents have an dominated effect on the red shift emission of CQDs. According to the EPR characterization and above structural characterization results, the tunable emission of CQDs in present work is probably ascribed to the increase of CQDs surface oxidation and carboxyl. The higher surface oxidation and carboxyl provide CQDs with more surface defects, resulting in narrower band gaps [34].Thus, the emission of CQDs shifts from blue to red gradually due to the increase of surface oxidation and carboxyl, the corresponding luminescence mechanism of multi-color fluorescent CQDs is also illustrated in Fig. 3(c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The properties of solid state CQDs@SiO2 phosphors
ce:section.ce:para: In most previous works, CQDs usually showed aggregation-induced luminescence quenching, resulting in low or quenching luminescence in solid state. So it is difficult to prepare solid state CQDs phosphors with excellent luminescence properties. In our present work, at the beginning, we prepared the solid state CQDs phosphors by drying the CQDs solution directly. However, due to the change of CQD surface state environment, no obvious emission was detected in solid state CQDs. In order to achieve CQD-based phosphors with good emission properties and controllable morphology, solid state CQDs@SiO2 phosphors were tried to prepare, remaining the CQD surface state environment unchanged. Fig. 4 (a) shows the photographs of CQDs@SiO2 phosphors under 365 nm UV light. The color of emission shifts from blue to red. In Fig. 4(b), PL emission spectra of CQDs and CQDs@SiO2 phosphors show that CQDs@SiO2 phosphors possess almost the same emission peaks with the corresponding CQDs solution, indicating the well-preserved luminescence properties of CQDs@SiO2 phosphors. In the following section, Y-CQDs@SiO2 phosphors were chosen as the representative sample for further characterizations. The TEM images of Y-CQDs@SiO2 phosphors are shown in Fig. 4(c–e). The Y-CQDs@SiO2 particles are well-dispersed in shape of sphere. The size distribution histograms are exhibited in the insets, which show that the average size of Y-CQDs@SiO2 phosphors becomes larger from 18 nm to 68 nm, and 159 nm with the increase of TEOS dosage. This is due to the fact that more TEOS molecules react with the oxhydryl on CQDs surface. XRD patterns of Y-CQDs@SiO2 phosphors are shown in Fig. 4(f), the broad peaks at 22° are assigned to the amorphous silica. The diffractive intensity enhances slightly with the increase of TEOS dosage. Fig. 4(g) exhibits the FTIR spectra of Y-CQDs and Y-CQDs@SiO2 phosphors. Broad bands at 1093 cm−1 and 1200 cm−1 attributed to the stretching vibration of Si-O-Si and Si-O-C verify the formation of silica layer. The PL spectra of Y-CQDs and Y-CQDs@SiO2 phosphors are shown in Fig. 4(h). The emission peaks of Y-CQDs@SiO2 phosphors are all around 550 nm, in accordance with the fluorescence of Y-CQDs. And the emission intensity gradually decreases with the thickening of silica layer. According to the above results, the excellent luminescent and controllable morphological properties of Y-CQDs solution is well preserved in solid state CQDs@SiO2 phosphors, which will significantly expand their potential application areas.

To fabricate multi-color CQDs@SiO2 phosphors-based LEDs, the CQDs@SiO2 phosphors mixed with AB glue were added onto LED chips, and the solidified mixture was obtained after curing at 150 °C for 1 h. Then multi-color LEDs were fabricated by using 365 nm emitting LED chip as an excitation source. Fig. 5 (a) shows the photoluminescence photographs of almost full-color operating solid state CQDs@SiO2 phosphors-based LEDs. The emission spectra (Fig. 5(b)) exhibit the maximum emission peak of multi-color LEDs at 447 nm, 516 nm, 551 nm, 595 nm, and 623 nm, respectively. From the results of emission spectra, CIE color coordinates of the LEDs are calculated in Fig. 5(c), which almost cover the whole visible region. Based on the multi-color CQDs@SiO2 phosphors-based LEDs, WLED was fabricated by tuning the ratio of blue, green, and red CQDs@SiO2 phosphors. The blue, green, and red emission peaks (Fig. 5(d)) of the mixed phosphors on the 365 nm emitting chip are located at 441 nm, 536 nm, and 622 nm, respectively. CIE (Fig. 5(e)) of the WLED is calculated to be (0.3497, 0.3045), which is very close to the ideal white light region. The results indicate that WLED has been successfully obtained with blue, green, and red CQDs@SiO2 phosphors. The CCT is 4878 K and the CRI is 85.2. The WLED fabricated in this work shows potential applications in solid state lighting, etc.

In conclusion, we successfully synthesized multi-color emitting CQDs through hydrothermal method with CA and urea as the raw materials. The emission bands of CQDs can be continuously tuned by adjusting the reaction conditions such as changing the ratio of CA and urea, hydrothermal temperature, reaction time, and solvent. The various reaction conditions changed the degree of CQDs surface oxidation and carboxyl, and the emission of CQDs was gradually red shift with the increase of surface defects. As a result, the emission peak of CQDs shifted from 450 nm to 650 nm, almost covering the whole visible spectrum. In order to avoid aggregation-induced luminescence quenching of CQDs phosphors, silica was used to encapsulate the CQDs. We successfully obtained multi-color emission CQDs@SiO2 phosphors remaining the CQDs surface state environment unchanged through “reversed-phase micromulsion” preparation method, and good emission properties of CQDs were well preserved in the solid state CQDs@SiO2 phosphors. This preparation method is critical and effective to avoid the aggregation-induced luminescence quenching of CQDs. The size of Y-CQDs@SiO2 phosphors distributes in a large range (18 nm - 159 nm) with different TEOS dosages, and the phosphors have good dispersibility. Finally, we fabricated multi-color LEDs with above solid state CQDs@SiO2 phosphors. CQDs@SiO2 phosphors-based WLED was successfully fabricated by tuning the content ratio of blue, green, and red emitting CQDs@SiO2 phosphors with CIE of (0.3497, 0.3045) and CRI of 85.2. The solid state CQDs@SiO2 phosphors and WLED are promising to be applied in solid state lighting, etc.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51772101), the National Key R&D Program of China (YS2018YFB110012), the Guangdong Natural Science Foundation for Distinguished Young Scholars (S2014A030306045), the Science and Technology Project of Guangdong Province (2017A010103037), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities, Local Innovative and Research Teams Project of Guangdong Pearl River Talents Program (2017BT01X137), and the Program for Innovative Research Teams at the University of the Ministry of Education of China (IRT_17R38).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A universal strategy for activating the multi‐color room‐temperature afterglow of carbon dots in a boric acid matrix
Angew. Chem. Int. Ed.

Nano Today

J. Mater. Chem. C

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bright multicolor bandgap fluorescent carbon quantum dots for electroluminescent light-emitting diodes
Adv. Mater.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High color rendering index trichromatic white and red LEDs prepared from silane-functionalized carbon dots
